topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the mundane flow of my afternoon. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that scraped against my ribs. It was a listing. My childhood home. Listed for sale. My fingers, slick with sudden cold sweat, hovered over the screen, unable to process the words: 'For Sale.' A dizzying wave washed over me, making the linoleum floor feel suddenly unsteady beneath my bare feet. I backed away from the monitor, stumbling over a rug, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my sternum. Every shadow in the hallway seemed to deepen, stretching and twisting into shapes that weren't quite right. I needed to look away, to check the windows, but my gaze kept snapping back to the listing, a morbid fascination pulling me closer to the abyss.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels swam before my eyes. The air in the small apartment grew thick, heavy, like wading through syrup. My hands were shaking so violently I had to press them against my thighs to steady them. *No. This isn't real.* The thought echoed hollowly in my mind, a fragile shield against the creeping sense of dread that coiled in my gut. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor, a sound that seemed deafening in the silence. I walked to the window, pulling back the curtain just enough to peer out at the street below. Nothing seemed amiss, yet the knowledge that something fundamental had shifted—that the past was now being offered up for transaction—made my skin crawl. I couldn't move; I was rooted to the spot, every muscle locked in a rigid tension.

[story 3]

The email subject line was simple: 'Property Opportunity.' But the content felt like a physical blow. I read it twice, then a third time, my vision blurring slightly at the edges. A coldness spread from my core outward, numbing my extremities. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white, the cheap veneer digging painfully into my palms. My throat constricted, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I stood up slowly, deliberately, as if moving too quickly might shatter the fragile illusion that I was still in control. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming inside my chest. I took a shaky step toward the door, my intention to flee overwhelming any rational thought.
